title: Fiber gateway shows red LOS light
summary: Troubleshoot a fiber modem or optical network terminal showing a red LOS light, blinking LOS alarm, or complete optical signal loss.
symptoms: LOS light is red or blinking
router Wi-Fi is up but WAN is down
common_causes: fiber patch cable is loose or bent
optical signal level out of range
resolution_steps: Inspect the fiber patch cable and make sure it is not pinched, sharply bent, or partially unplugged.
Power cycle the ONT and gateway for 60 seconds.
Check the outage dashboard for the serving area.
If LOS stays red, schedule a field technician because customer-side resets do not restore optical light levels.
Advise the customer not to stare into the fiber port or clean connectors without approved tools.
escalation_policy: Escalate to field operations when the LOS alarm persists after power cycling and outage checks.

title: Home internet speed drops every evening
summary: Differentiate between Wi-Fi congestion inside the home and actual access network congestion when a customer reports evening slowdown.
symptoms: speed is normal in the morning but slow at night
wired tests are faster than Wi-Fi tests
common_causes: background streaming or gaming on the local network
resolution_steps: Run separate wired and Wi-Fi speed tests to isolate LAN versus WAN performance.
Move high-bandwidth devices to 5 GHz or Ethernet where possible.
Reposition mesh nodes and reduce overlap with neighboring networks.
Review gateway diagnostics for channel congestion and packet loss during the affected window.
If wired performance is also degraded, open a network capacity ticket with timestamps and test results.
escalation_policy: Escalate to broadband network operations only when wired speed tests confirm peak-hour degradation.

Set bridge mode on managed gateway

title: Set bridge mode on managed gateway
summary: Explain bridge mode for customers who want to use their own router, including what features stop working and how to avoid double NAT.
symptoms: customer wants to use personal router
VPN or port forwarding behaves inconsistently
common_causes: managed gateway is routing and NATing traffic
personal router is connected LAN-to-LAN instead of WAN-to-LAN
customer expects mesh features from both routers simultaneously
resolution_steps: Confirm the gateway model supports bridge or IP passthrough mode.
Enable bridge mode or IP passthrough according to the supported device workflow.
Move the customer router connection to the correct WAN port.
Explain that parental controls, integrated mesh, and some phone features may stop working on the managed gateway.
Reboot both devices after the mode change and verify the customer router receives the public IP.
escalation_policy: Escalate to advanced broadband support if bridge mode is unsupported on the installed gateway or voice services depend on routed mode.
